event.text: # Project Management Plan Update —

MAAP-1

**Document Control:** MAAP-1-PMP-UPDATE-001
**Revision

:** 1.0
**Classification:** Program Controlled
**Applies To:** All Functional Leads,

Program Control Board, Customer Representatives
**Triggered By:** Proposed Change —
Production Rate Acceleration, LRIP-2 (2 →

6 Aircraft/Year)
**Authority:** Program Manager, MAAP-1

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update documents the programmatic impact
, required baseline revisions, risk posture changes, and recommended path forward resulting
from the proposed production rate acceleration for the Multi-Mission Autonomous Aircraft Program (MAAP-1). The proposed
change — tripling annual production throughput from 2 to 6 aircraft per year beginning in
Low-Rate Initial Production Lot 2 (LRIP-2) — constit
utes a Class I change under the program's configuration management authority.
It affects cost, schedule, technical baseline, supply chain architecture
, quality posture, and workforce planning simultaneously, and therefore requires a formal

PMP update before any implementation authority is granted.

This document
does not authorize the proposed change. It characterizes the change, establishes the analytical
basis for the Program Control Board (PCB) decision, and defines the conditional

implementation framework if the change is approved.

### 1.2 Background and

Change Rationale

The MAAP-1 program is currently executing Engineering and Manufacturing Development (EMD),
with Critical Design Review (CDR) at program month (PM
) 42, first flight targeted at PM 54
, and FAA Type Certification at PM 84. The Low-Rate Initial Production authorization is base
lined at PM 72 (WBS 7.8), with LRIP Lot 1 comprising
3 aircraft over 32 weeks and a rate ramp-up activity
(WBS 7.8.2) allocated 16
weeks. The Full Rate Production (FRP) Decision is scheduled at PM 84–92,
preceded by Operational Test and Evaluation (OT&E) using aircraft

PA-1 through PA-6.

The proposed change is commercially motivated.
Wildfire fleet demand — principally from CA
L FIRE, Canadian provincial operators, and emerging international firef
ighting agencies — represents a documented market opportunity of 200+ aircraft over fifteen years in the firefighting segment alone
, with a base-case (P50) demand of 92
units through 2035 per the Customer Demand
Forecast. The MAAP-1F firefighting variant is the primary
vehicle for this market. The rate acceleration proposal responds to growing operator urgency and competitive

pressure.

However, the manufacturing, supply chain, financial, and quality analyses conducted
across all five program documents establish clearly that the program's read
iness posture at LRIP-2 is fundamentally incompatible with a 3× rate increase applied without
preconditions. Manufacturing readiness is insufficient, supply chain contracts are sized for baseline
throughput, learning curve benefits have not been captured, O
T&E and FRP decision gates remain open, and the MAAP-1F FA
A certification basis for autonomous firefighting operations has not been closed. On a
Firm Fixed Price (FFP) contract structure, cost growth
from premature rate acceleration is absorbed entirely by program margin.
A critical internal document inconsistency must also be acknowledged at
the outset. The Manufacturing Ramp Plan already specifies 6 aircraft per year as
the LRIP-2 baseline rate, planned for 2027, with the
LRIP-1 rate established at 2 aircraft per year for 2025–
2026. The Supply Chain Procurement Plan similarly reflects 12
engines (6 aircraft) for LRIP-2. However, the IMS baseline
documents LRIP production at approximately 1 aircraft per 10
weeks (~5.2/year) with LRIP
Lot 1 at 3 aircraft over 32 weeks, and several risk register entries
are sized against a lower baseline demand. This inconsistency must be formally reconc
iled by the Configuration Control Board (CCB) before any LRIP-2 rate decision is made. The impact
assessments in this PMP Update proceed on the interpretation most consistent with the greatest risk
exposure: that the proposed change represents either (a) a compression or
elimination of the LRIP-1 stabilization period, or (b) an acceleration of the LRIP-2 start date beyond
what the program's current readiness state supports, regardless

of whether 6/year was always the planned LRIP-2 target.

### 1.3 Document

Scope and Authority

This PMP Update applies to all program functional areas. It
supersedes conflicting guidance in the program baseline for topics addressed
herein and is effective upon Program Manager signature. Implementation of
any production rate change described in this document requires separate PCB approval with

documented exit criteria satisfaction.

---

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Change Scope Definition

The proposed change modifies the following program scope elements:

**Production Rate:** LRIP-2 annual throughput increases
from 2 to 6 aircraft per year, a 3× step increase applied before OT&E completion,
before the FRP decision gate, and before MAAP-1F FAA certification is

closed for autonomous firefighting operations.

**Manufacturing Scope:** Achieving 6
aircraft per year requires activation of a second Final Assembly Line (FAL) flow
, installation and qualification of a second autoclave (currently approved
but undelivered), potential addition of a third assembly
station (currently a contingency, not a baseline
), expanded tooling and fixtures, and two-shift operations. All of
these actions must transition from contingency or F

RP-phase plans to LRIP-phase commitments.

**Supply Chain
Scope:** Long-term agreements with Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers must be renegotiated to reflect
6/year volume. Current agreements were sized for 2/year
LRIP throughput. The 200% rate increase exce
eds existing contractual rate-increase options (c
apped at 50% with 12-month notice) for most suppliers, necessitating formal contract
modifications. Supplier capacity expansion investments — including propulsion
system suppliers requiring a 24-month expansion timeline — must be funded

and underway before LRIP-2 rate commitment.

**Workforce Scope:** The production
workforce must grow from the 150-person L
RIP-1 complement to 280 persons (Workforce Hiring Wave 2) at
minimum, with the hiring wave initiated 9 months before LRIP-2 start.
Specialized high-voltage and hybrid propulsion technician
shortfalls (RISK-M-004) must be resolved
before rate increase. The 85,000+ training hours planned for the production

workforce cannot be compressed without quality consequences.

**Quality Scope:** The Quality Management Plan's
LRIP-1 first-time quality (FTQ) target
of 90% must be achieved and validated before LRIP-2 rate increase. At
6 aircraft per year, a 90% FTQ rate generates rework burden on 10
% of assemblies at three times the baseline volume, creating
a rework cascade incompatible with delivery commitments on an imm
ature line. The 90-day observation hold on first
production aircraft creates delivery pipeline conflicts at the acceler

ated rate.

**Financial Scope:** The $74M management reserve (1.5% of contract
value), sized for the baseline ramp, is insufficient
to absorb the cost exposure created by rate acceleration. Capital expenditure for LRIP-2 preparation
($52M in the Manufacturing Ramp Plan baseline) may need
to be front-loaded into earlier periods, and total capital commitment could
reach $120M in a single year, competing with the $68M already

planned for LRIP-1 activation.

**Certification Scope:** MAAP-1F firef
ighting variant aircraft shall not be delivered to wildfire operators
in quantities that exceed demonstrated FAA regulatory authorization
for the intended mission profile. LRIP-2 aircraft produced
before the MAAP-1F certification basis is closed carry retrofit exposure if any
post-LRIP-2 certification finding requires design modification.

### 2.2 Scope Exclusions

This PMP Update does not authorize:
- Any increase in total

program aircraft quantity beyond the currently contracted baseline
- F
RP-phase rate increases beyond the 24 aircraft/year FRP target
- Changes to the MAAP-1E or MAAP-1R variant production sched

ules
- Relaxation of DAL A/B software qualification
requirements, DO-178C compliance, or safety-critical system acceptance

test coverage at any production rate

### 2.3 Baseline

Documents Requiring Revision

The following program documents require formal revision
if the proposed change is approved. Document-level impact assessments conducted across all five analyzed documents establish

these revision requirements:

| Document | Revision Required | Priority |
|---|

---|---|
| maap1_integrated_master_schedule.md | Add LRIP Lot 2 WBS element
; revise rate ramp-up duration; re-run critical path analysis;

update resource loading | Immediate |
| maap1_risk_register.md | Res
SC-001 through SC-006, C-002; add
new risks RISK-M-008, RISK-C-005, RISK-SC-007

| Immediate |
| maap1_program_requirements_baseline.md | Add MRL gate
criteria; re-validate Constraint C-1 cost model
; add MAAP-1F certification-gated delivery clause;

conduct Rate Acceleration Trade Study | Near-term |
| maap1_financial
_and_contract_management.md | Re-baseline production cost model; rev
ise learning curve assumption; update cash flow table; increase management reserve;

model updated downside scenarios | Near-term |
| maap1_supply_chain_procurement_plan.md | Rev
ise engine and transmission procurement sections; update ramp gate Go
/No-Go criteria; add Rate Readiness Index metric; document 200% rate increase beyond 50

% contractual ceiling | Immediate |
| maap1_bill_of_materials.md | Add Production Rate
Assumptions section; update NRC tooling baseline; revise critical
path mitigation strategies for 6/year; re-adj

udicate make/buy decisions | Near-term |
| maap1_customer_demand_forecast.md | Revise IMS milestone table; elev
ate AS-014 risk rating; add demand absorption/
inventory risk entry; restate Section 11.

3 final assessment | Near-term |
| maap1_program_overview.md | Add LRIP lot structure and M
RL targets to §6.1; revise unit price sensitivity narrative; add

production ramp risk acknowledgment | Near-term |

---

## 3. Milestone List & Schedule Baseline

### 3.1 Baseline Schedule

Context

The IMS critical path carries zero days of float across
its 92-month span. Key baselined milestones governing

the production ramp decision are:

| Milestone | IMS Reference | Baseline Date |

|---|---|---|
| Production Process Validation Complete | WBS 7.7.

1–7.7.4 | November 2033 |
| Production Readiness Review | WBS 7.7.4

| November 2033 |
| LRIP Authorization | WBS 7.8 |

16-Nov-33 |
| LRIP Lot 1 (3 aircraft,

32 weeks) | WBS 7.8.1 | Nov 2033 – Jul 2034 |
| Rate R

amp-Up Activity | WBS 7.8.2 | 16 weeks concurrent |
| IOC | WBS 7.9

.4 | 25-Jul-34 |
| OT&E Period (PA
-1 through PA-6) | TEMP Section 12.1 | PM 72–

84 |
| FRP Decision Review | WBS 7.10 | 19-Sep

-34 |
| FRP Authorization | WBS 7.10.3 | PM 84–92 |

### 3.2 Schedule Impact

of Proposed Change

Adding LRIP-2 at 6 aircraft per year before the FRP decision introduces new critical path activities
with durations of 12–40 weeks that are not currently scheduled
. The following new activities become hard prerequisites to LRIP-2 rate authorization
, each with fixed minimum durations driven by physical and contract

ual constraints:

| Prerequisite Activity | Minimum Lead Before LRIP-2 | Nature |

|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 facility expansion | 18 months | Construction — not schedule

-elastic |
| Second autoclave delivery and qualification | 14–18 months | Capital

equipment procurement |
| Additional tooling order (airframe, composite
, FAL) | 14 months (48-week fabrication +

12-week qualification) | Hard lead time |
| Workforce Hiring Wave 2 (
LRIP-1 → LRIP-2 increment) | 9 months minimum | Recruiting, on

boarding, qualification |
| Supplier contract modifications (engine, transmission, avionics) | 12

months (rate-increase notice requirement) | Contractual |
| MRL-8
achievement confirmation | Gate — must precede rate authority

| Regulatory/program governance |
| LRIP-1 composite layup yield
≥82% sustained for 3 months | Gate — must precede rate authority |

Quality prerequisite |

A schedule risk assessment places the aggregate
slip potential from these prerequisite gaps at 4–
12 months on IOC and the FRP Decision
Review, if the rate increase is attempted without satisf
ying the prerequisites. An IOC slip of this
magnitude would push IOC from July 2034 to the November 2034 –
July 2035 window, with corresponding FRP delay — directly underm

ining the market capture rationale for acceleration.

### 3.3 Required IMS Rev

isions

The following IMS modifications are required before LRIP-2 rate authority can be formally considered:

**New W
BS Element — 7.8.4, LRIP Lot 2:**
Define scope, aircraft quantity, duration, start/finish
dates, and all predecessor/successor dependencies. Integrate into critical path analysis with

zero-float tracking restored.

**Revised WBS 7.8.2 (Rate Ramp-Up)
:** Extend duration and resequence to reflect the
higher target rate. The current 16-week allocation is insufficient for a

3× ramp.

**Revised §12.3 Resource Loading:** Update
peak manufacturing staff from 450 to reflect accelerated hiring,
QA personnel from 75 to reflect proportional scaling, and total
manufacturing hours from 3.2M to reflect 6/

year throughput.

**Critical Path Re-run:** All additions to the LRIP phase
must be re-evaluated for IOC (WBS 7.9.4) and FRP Decision (WBS 7.10.3) cascade
impact. Schedule acceleration opportunities elsewhere in the network must be identified to
preserve the IOC date, or IOC/FRP dates

must be formally re-baselined through PCB.

### 3.4 Proposed Rate

Gate Milestone Framework

This PMP Update establishes a gated rate increase
framework as the authorized implementation mechanism if the proposed change is approved by
PCB. No LRIP-2 rate authority above 2 aircraft/year is granted until all
gates below are passed and documented. Gates are evaluated
sequentially; failure of any gate holds the rate at the preceding level

.

| Gate | Criterion | Target Validation Point |
|---|---|---|
| Gate 1 —
Autoclave Readiness | Second autoclave operational, qualified, and producing conform
ing composite parts for 30 days continuous | Before LRIP-2 composite

demand peaks |
| Gate 2 — Composite Yield | Composite layup first-
pass yield ≥82% sustained over 3 consecutive months | After aircraft #3 delivery

(LRIP-1) |
| Gate 3 — Supply Chain Contracts | All Tier 1 and critical Tier 2 suppliers formally
contracted at 6/year volumes; capacity confirmed in writing

| 6 months before LRIP-2 start |
| Gate 4 — FAL Third
Station | Third assembly station installed, flow-balanced, and flow
time validated at ≤10 weeks per aircraft | Before LRIP

-2 authorization |
| Gate 5 — Workforce | EV/HV techn
ician headcount at 1.5:1 ratio for all
LRIP-2 stations; all personnel training-complete | Before LRIP-

2 workforce activation |
| Gate 6 — Quality Reserve | Retrofit reserve
fund increased from $2M to minimum $5M; QA staffing plan

validated for 6/year throughput | Before LRIP-2 authorization |
| Gate 7 — MRL Assessment | Independent
Manufacturing Readiness Level assessment confirms MRL ≥ 8 against
SAE AS2805 criteria at 6/year rate

| At LRIP-1 exit |
| Gate 8 — MAAP-1F Certification | FAA certification basis closed
for firefighting variant; no open certification findings requiring design action

| Before LRIP-2 aircraft delivered to wildfire customers |

---

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1

Change Classification

The proposed production rate acceleration meets the threshold for a **Class I Engineering
Change** under the program's Configuration Management authority (W

BS 8.2.4). The classification basis is:

- **Schedule
Impact:** Greater than 30 days on multiple IMS milestones; potential

IOC slip of 4–12 months
- **Cost Impact:** Estimated incremental cost exposure of $91
M–$199M (probable range), with upper-bound scenarios

approaching $319M
- **Contract Scope:** Rate increase of 200%
exceeds existing contractual rate-increase options (capped at 50%) with most Tier 1 suppliers, requiring contract

modifications
- **Technical Baseline:** Tooling, facility, and staffing changes required
; make/buy decisions require re-adjudication; configuration management burden

increases with 6 aircraft in concurrent production
- **Customer Impact
:** Delivery schedule changes to wildfire fleet customers; potential for delivery of

aircraft ahead of contracted demand; MAAP-1F certification dependency

### 4.2 Change Control

Board Process

1. **Program Manager** submits formal Change

Request with this PMP Update as the supporting analysis document
2. **CC
B Review** — minimum attendees: PM, Chief Engineer
, Manufacturing Director, Supply Chain Director, Chief Financial Officer, Quality Director, Contracts Lead, Customer Representative
3. **PCB Decision** — approve, approve with conditions (gated framework

per Section 3.4), or reject
4. **Baseline Revision** — if approved, all eight documents
identified in Section 2.3 are formally revised per the

Document Control process within 60 days of PCB approval
5. **Customer
Notification** — Contract modification negotiations initiated with wildfire fleet customers within 30 days of PC
B approval; no delivery commitments made at accelerated rate until contract

modifications are executed

### 4.3 Change Communication Plan

| Audience | Message | Timing | Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program Control Board | Full PMP Update — risk, cost, schedule, quality implications

| Immediate | Formal PCB package |
| Manufacturing Director | Gate criteria, tool

ing and facility investment requirements | Within 7 days | Functional lead briefing |
| Supply Chain Director
| Immediate supplier notification actions (Section 7) | Within 7 days |

Functional lead briefing |
| Customer (CAL FIRE, etc.) | Production
capacity study underway; no commitment until PCB approval and

contract modification | Within 14 days | Program Manager letter |
| Corporate Finance | Reserve aug
mentation request; capital expenditure front-loading implications | Within 14 days | EA

C revision brief |
| FAA (as applicable) | No
regulatory communication until MAAP-1F certification path is confirmed | As

determined by Legal/Contracts | Formal channel |

### 4.4 Conditions

Applicable to All Rate Increase Decisions

Regardless of PCB decision, the following conditions apply

to any production rate increase in the LRIP phase:

- **Demand must be contract
ually secured before rate increase is authorized.** Market demand forecasts and letters
of intent do not constitute sufficient basis for rate authority. Firm, funded customer contracts
must be in hand, with milestone payment terms that recover the cost

of acceleration.
- **OT&E findings must be assessed before LRIP-2 deliveries to operators
.** Aircraft delivered before OT&E closes carry retrofit risk if findings
require design correction. The program shall maintain a retrofit reserve and

design freeze protocol consistent with LRIP-2 delivery commitments.
- **The
MAAP-1F certification-gated delivery constraint (Gate 8) is non-negotiable.** Delivering
aircraft to firefighting operators before the FAA certification basis is closed for autonomous operations
creates regulatory and safety exposure that the program cannot accept

. This constraint is treated as equivalent to a program safety requirement.

---

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Current Financial Baseline

The program's approved financial baseline establ

ishes:

- **Total Program Cost:** $4,380M
- **Total

Program Revenue:** $4,778M
- **Gross Margin (Baseline):** $

398M (9.1%)
- **Management Reserve:** $74M (1.5%

of contract value)
- **FRP Unit Price Constraint (PRB
C-1):** ≤$21.5M per aircraft at FRP Lot 5 (F

Y25$)
- **Current EVM Status
:** 3% unfavorable cost variance (RISK-C
-001, rated Critical, score 16) — the
program is already carrying negative cost performance before the proposed

change is implemented
- **Learning Curve Assumption:** 85% (conservative vs

. industry 82%)
- **LRIP-2 Unit Cost Reduction

Target:** 12% from LRIP-1 average

### 5.2 Cost Impact of

Proposed Change

The proposed rate acceleration creates cost growth across six primary drivers.
The ranges below represent probable (not worst-case) outcomes based

on cross-document financial analysis:

| Cost Driver | Estimated Impact | Confidence |

Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Facility and tooling acceleration (pull
-forward of FRP-phase capital) | +$50M – $
80M | Medium | Manufacturing Ramp Plan; IMS Section 10.4

|
| Supply chain expedite premiums and contract renegot
iation | +$25M – $45M | Medium-High | RISK-SC-002 spot
-market premium (15%); engine expedite scenario

($48M) |
| Learning curve degradation (90% actual vs. 85% plan
) | +$60M – $125M | Medium | Financial model scenario; pro-rated to

LRIP impact |
| Overtime, shift premium, and surge labor costs | +$15
M – $25M | High | RISK-M-004 technician premium; two-shift

contingency becoming baseline |
| Composite structures BUY penalty
(delayed make transition) | +$3M – $4M per year at
6 units | High | $565K/aircraft cost
differential (BOM: $1,850K buy

vs. $1,285K internal) |
| Quality escapes, rework, sc
rap, and retrofit reserve shortfall | +$20M – $40M | Medium | RISK-M-005
; $2M reserve undersized at 3×

volume |
| **Total Probable Incremental Cost Exposure** | **+

$173M – $319M** | | |

The midpoint of this range (~$245M) represents
approximately 5.6% of the $4,380M program cost baseline. Applied
against the baseline $398M gross margin, the midpoint exposure alone reduces
gross margin from 9.1% to approximately 4.1% — below most
corporate hurdle rates and well below program target.
At the upper bound (+$319M), the program approaches breakeven or
loss territory, consistent with the modeled "Perfect Storm

" scenario outcome (-1.2% gross margin).

### 5.3 Cost Constraint C-1 Reass

essment

The PRB Constraint C-1 (≤$21.5M/aircraft at FRP Lot 5) was prem
ised on a learning curve derived from the baseline production ramp. Acceler
ating LRIP-2 before manufacturing processes are stable may improve
the FRP cost trajectory if executed cleanly — the unit
cost reduction from volume production is real. However, if
the ramp outpaces readiness (the high-probability scenario given
the identified gaps), increased rework, scrap, overtime, and exped
ite costs in LRIP-2 will flatten the learning curve and erode the cost baseline from which
FRP pricing is projected. A formal re-validation of C-1 against
a revised learning curve model reflecting 6/year

LRIP-2 production must be completed before PCB approval of the rate increase.

### 5.4 Learning Curve Corrective Action Protocol
A formal learning curve tracking dashboard shall be established with monthly reporting. The following

protocol applies regardless of PCB decision on the rate change:

- **Trigger Level
1 (Watch):** Actual learning curve exceeds 87% for

two consecutive months → Program Manager notification; root cause analysis initiated
- **Trigger Level 2 (Corrective Action
):** Actual learning curve exceeds 87% for three consecutive months → Rate increase suspended pending root cause and recovery

plan; mandatory PCB briefing
- **Trigger Level 3 (Rate Reduction):** Actual learning curve exceeds 90
% for any month → Production rate reduced to preceding rate level; independent assessment

required before rate is restored

### 5.5 Management Reserve Augmentation

The existing $74M management reserve is structurally insufficient
to absorb the midpoint cost exposure ($245M) identified above. If the PC

B approves the rate acceleration, the program shall:

1. Request corporate approval for
**$50M – $75M additional management reserve** specifically
designated for rate acceleration risk, bringing total reserve to $124M – $149M (

approximately 2.5–3.0% of contract value)
2. Frame this reserve aug
mentation as investment protection for the acceleration decision, not as acknowledgment of program

overrun
3. Maintain the reserve in a separately tracked cost account with
draw-down authorization requiring PM and CFO co

-signature

### 5.6 Cash Flow Implications

The current cumulative cash trough is
-$103M (2029). Rate acceleration front-loads
costs before deliveries generate milestone payments, deepening this

trough. Key cash flow actions:

- **Progress payment rate review:** Evaluate requesting increase
from 80% to 85% during the ramp surge period to

partially offset the cash flow impact of accelerated cost absorption
- **Milestone payment acceleration
:** Negotiate with wildfire fleet customers for earlier milestone payment triggers (e.g., delivery
acceptance at factory rather than customer site acceptance) to improve

cash timing
- **Capital expenditure staging:** Do not commit to the full $52
M LRIP-2 preparation investment in a single fiscal year; stage against

gate criteria in Section 3.4 to preserve capital optionality

### 5.

7 Should-Cost Modeling Requirement

Before committing to the
rate increase, the program shall complete bottoms-up should-cost modeling for LRIP-2 and
LRIP-3 at 6 aircraft per year, validated against supplier quotes with not
-to-exceed (NTE) caps. Parametric estimates alone are insufficient authorization
basis for an FFP commitment of this scope.

---

## 6. Risk Management Plan & Risk Register

### 6.1 Risk Governance Changes
The proposed change expands the program risk population and elev

ates several existing risks to Critical status. The following governance changes are effective immediately:

- **Risk Review Cad
ence:** Monthly risk reviews elevated to bi-weekly for all

risks rated High or Critical
- **Rate Acceleration Risk Category:** A
new risk category, "Rate Acceleration," is established in the risk register to aggregate

and report on risks created or elevated by this change
- **Executive Watch
List:** Risks rated Critical (score ≥16) are added to the corporate executive program

watch list with monthly reporting

### 6.2 Existing Risks — Revised Scores
The following risk register entries require rescoring to reflect the rate
acceleration impact. Revised scores represent the risk posture if the change is implemented without

preconditions:

| Risk ID | Title | Current Score | Revised Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| RISK-M-001 | Composite Lay
up Yield Shortfall | 12 (High) | **16 (Critical)** | 70
% first-pass yield at 3× volume generates 1
.8 aircraft/year of rework; contingency (slow

to 2/month) directly conflicts with proposed change |
| RISK-M
-002 | Autoclave Capacity Bottleneck | 12 (High) | **16 (Critical)** | Single autoclave cannot sust

Supplier Financial Instability | 8 (Medium) | **12 (High)** | Accelerated demand st
resses small supplier cash flow; $500K bridge

### 6.3 New

Risks — Rate Acceleration Category

The following risks are created by the proposed change and are not
currently captured in the register. All require formal entry
with assigned owners, response plans, and monitoring protocols

:

**RISK-M-008: Production Rate Exceeds Manufacturing Readiness Level

**
- **Description:** Committing to 6 aircraft/year in LRIP-2 before achieving M
RL-8 creates systemic risk across composite fabrication, FAL through
put, and workforce capacity simultaneously. No single existing risk captures the integrated

rate-versus-readiness gap.
- **Probability:** High | **

Consequence:** High | **Score: 16 (Critical)**
- **Owner:** Manufacturing Director
- **Mitigation:** Conduct formal MRL assessment per SAE AS2805 criteria as
a mandatory gate prerequisite; minimum MRL-8 required before any rate

above 2/year is authorized

**RISK-C-005: Learning Curve Disruption from

Premature Rate Acceleration**
- **Description:** Moving to 6 aircraft/year before process stabil
ization flattens the 85% learning curve assumption, potentially increasing LRIP-2 unit costs
by 20–35% above target and permanently elevating the FRP cost baseline

- **Probability:** High | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 12 (High)**
- **Owner:** Chief Financial Officer
- **Mitigation:** Monthly
learning curve tracking per Section 5.4; automatic rate freeze trigger at 87% sustained act

uals

**RISK-SC-007: Supply Chain Not Contracted for Accelerated Rate**
- **Description:** Current long-term agreements sized for 2/year contain 50
% rate-increase options — insufficient for a 200% increase. Contract
ual gaps create allocation shortfalls and potential supplier claims simultaneously

across multiple Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers
- **Probability:** High | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 12 (High)**
- **Owner:** Supply

Chain Director
- **Mitigation:** Immediate contract-by-contract review; formal rate increase notifications
; contract modifications executed before Gate 3 is declared satisfied
**RISK-NEW-01: OT&E Findings Trigger Retrofit of

Accelerated LRIP-2 Aircraft**
- **Description:** Aircraft delivered before OT&E closes carry retrofit risk if operational testing identifies
design or manufacturing deficiencies requiring fleet-wide correction.
At 6 aircraft/year, a larger delivered fleet amplifies retrofit cost

and operational disruption
- **Probability:** High | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 16 (Critical)**
- **Owner:**

Chief Engineer / Test Director
- **Mitigation:** Maintain design freeze protocol; retrofit
reserve at $5M minimum; do not deliver LRIP-2 aircraft to

operators until OT&E major findings are dispositioned

**RISK-NEW-02: MA

AP-1F Certification Delay Creates Stranded Asset Inventory**
- **Description:** Producing 6
MAAP-1F aircraft per year before FAA authorization for autonomous
firefighting operations is obtained leaves completed aircraft with no legal
delivery pathway, generating inventory carrying costs and potential retrofit

requirements for any certification-driven design changes
- **Probability:**

Medium | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 12 (High)**
- **Owner:** Certification Director /

Program Manager
- **Mitigation:** Gate 8 (MAAP-1F certification closure) is non-negotiable prerequisite to
firefighting variant deliveries; maintain storage and preservation capability for

up to 6 aircraft during certification completion period

**RISK-NEW-03: Quality Escape
in Wildfire Operations — Reputational and Safety Catastroph

e**
- **Description:** An early production quality escape on a MAAP-1F aircraft operating
in wildfire conditions (Category I safety consequence — loss
of aircraft) would destroy the wildfire market opportunity the acceleration
is intended to capture, trigger regulatory intervention, and generate liability exposure disproportionate to any

revenue gain from accelerated production
- **Probability:** Low-Medium | **Consequence:**

Catastrophic | **Score: 12 (High)**
- **Owner:** Chief Engineer / Quality Director
- **Mitigation:** MRL-8 gate before
rate increase; maintain 90-day observation hold on all LRIP-2 aircraft before wildfire deployment
; DO-178C DAL A compliance non

-negotiable at any rate

**RISK-NEW-04: Critical Path Float Consumed;

IOC and FRP Dates Slip**
- **Description:** The IMS baseline
carries zero critical path float. New prerequisite activities for rate
acceleration (facility expansion, tooling, autoclave qualification) add parallel
-path pressures with fixed durations, consuming all remaining

schedule margin and extending IOC and FRP dates
- **Probability:** High | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 12

(High)**
- **Owner:** Program Manager / Scheduler
- **Mitigation:** Do not begin rate acceleration activities without
dedicated resources that are additions to, not substitutions

from, existing critical path resources; re-run critical path analysis immediately

## 7. Procurement / Supply Chain Management Plan

### 7.1 Immediate Supplier Actions

(0–30 Days)

The following actions are required immediately, regardless of PCB decision on
the rate change. These are information-gathering and contract
ual notice actions that do not commit the program to the
rate increase but preserve optionality and satisfy notice period obligations

:

1. **Reconcile internal document conflict.** The
Supply Chain Procurement Plan reflects 12 engines (
6 aircraft) for LRIP-2, while other program documents reflect
lower LRIP-2 demand. A CCB-reviewed reconciliation must establish

the authoritative baseline before any supplier notification is issued.

2. **Issue
formal capacity inquiry to GE Aviation (T700
engines, 52+ week lead time).** Confirm whether
LRIP-2 quantity of 12 engines is already under contract and whether delivery
cadence aligns to any proposed accelerated start date.
The program's contractual rate-increase option allows
50% quantity increase with 12-month notice — a

200% increase requires contract modification and must be initiated immediately.

3. **Issue
emergency capacity review notice to main transmission supplier (
Supplier X, 40-week casting lead time).** The transmission is a
sole-source, clean-sheet design with no buffer inventory planned
until LRIP-3. Supplier X is still in the N
RE/qualification phase. Immediate casting pre-buy
orders for a 6-month buffer must be evaluated
and, if justified, placed pending PCB rate decision

.

4. **Survey all Tier 1 and critical Tier 2 suppliers** against a 6/year demand
signal. Require written capacity confirmation or identification of expansion investment
and timeline required to support 6/year. This
survey provides the evidence base for Gate 3 in the rate gate framework

.

5. **Issue 12-month rate increase notifications** to all suppliers where
6/year LRIP-2 is not already contracted. This preserves the contract
ual notice clock regardless of PCB decision and avoids further erosion of the available lead

time.

### 7.2 Long-Lead Item Procurement Strategy

Long-lead items governing LRIP-2 production
must have purchase orders in the pipeline before assembly demand material
izes. At a 52-week maximum lead time, engines for the
6th LRIP-2 aircraft must be on order before the first LRIP-1 aircraft is delivered. The

following schedule governs acceptable procurement action timing:

| Item | Lead Time | Required
PO Date for 6/Year LRIP-2 | Current

PO Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| GE T700 Engine Assembly | 52+ weeks | 12 months before each

assembly start | Confirm immediately |
| Main Transmission Housing (
castings) | 40 weeks | 10 months before each assembly start | Issue casting orders immediately

|
| AESA Radar System | 48 weeks | 12 months before av

ionics installation | Confirm/place |
| Main Landing Gear Forging | 40 weeks |

10 months before landing gear installation | Pre-buy revision required |
| Ejection Seat Assembly | 44

weeks | 11 months before seat installation | Confirm capacity |
| Flight Control Computer | 40 weeks | 10 months before avionics installation | Verify against

60-unit order coverage |
| APU (Honeywell GT
CP36-150) | 36 weeks | 9 months before installation | Standard

scheduling |

### 7.3 Contract Strategy Revisions

**Rate-
Increase Options Ceiling:** Existing supplier contracts cap rate-increase options at 50% with 12
-month advance notice. The proposed 200% rate increase (2 to 6 aircraft/
year) universally exceeds this ceiling for suppliers contracted at the 2/year baseline. Formal
contract modifications, not option exercises, are required. Budget negoti
ation authority must be allocated for potential pricing premium conc

essions required to secure supplier agreement to accelerated volumes.

**Multi-Year Fixed
-Price Commitment Strategy:** The program's existing multi-year fixed-price contract strategy
(exemplified by the $185M rotor blade set commitment) is
the correct model to replicate across the accelerated supply base
. Long-term fixed-price commitments at 6/year volumes provide cost certainty, lock
in pricing before demand signal drives spot-market premiums, and give

suppliers the revenue confidence to fund capacity expansion investments.

**MAAP-1F Variant
Supply Chain:** The firefighting variant includes mission-specific
equipment (retardant delivery systems, firefighting sensors, terrain mapping
) not in the baseline BOM. These represent Class I change
exposure of $20M–$50M in engineering and qualification costs. If not
captured in a contract modification with the wildfire fleet customer, they
erode margin directly. A separate contract line item or modification
for MAAP-1F variant NRE and qualification costs must be negotiated before LRIP-2 firef

ighting-variant production is authorized.

### 7.4 Make/Buy Re-Adj

udication

The make/buy strategy established in the Manufacturing Ramp Plan and BOM must
be formally re-adjudicated for all items currently designated MAKE against available

internal capacity at 6 aircraft/year. Key findings from initial analysis:

- **Composite
fuselage barrels:** In-sourcing trigger remains at
12+ units/year (LRIP-3). At 6/
year, continue buying at $1,850K/set.

No make/buy change triggered.
- **CNC machined structures:** In
-sourcing trigger at >50 units/year total. Continue buying at 6/year. No

change.
- **Wiring harnesses:** In-house from LRIP-1. Capacity
validation for 6/year required — internal capacity audit mandated before

LRIP-2.
- **Final assembly:** In-house at all phases
; second assembly flow activation is the critical LRIP-2 production

action.

### 7.5 Supply Chain Risk Monitoring

A **weekly supplier
quality review** shall be added to the program management cadence during
the LRIP-1/LRIP-2 transition period, supplementing the existing weekly kit
shortage meeting. A **Rate Readiness Index**
metric shall be developed and reported monthly at PCB, tracking each

Tier 1 supplier's demonstrated capacity versus committed 6/year demand.

Any new
supplier qualifications required by rate acceleration must include full trusted supplier
program assessment per NIST 800-161, maintaining

the program's supply chain infiltration risk controls even under schedule pressure.

---

##

8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Quality Posture at Baseline vs. Accelerated Rate

The Quality
Management Plan establishes a deliberate quality maturation staircase prem
ised on process stabilization at each rate before advancement

to the next:

| Phase | FTQ Target | Cp

k Target | Supplier DPPM | Scrap/Rework |
|---|---|---|---|---|

| <500 | <1% |

The LRIP-1 FTQ target of 90% is intent
ional — it represents the expected quality level during process learning, not a failure
. The problem created by rate acceleration is volume: at 90
% FTQ and 6 aircraft/year, 10% of major assembl
ies require rework or rejection across three times the production volume
, generating a rework backlog that cascades through
the FAL schedule and consumes capacity that should be going

to productive assembly.

Achieving the LRIP-2 FTQ target of 95% requires that LRIP-1 quality
lessons are fully incorporated before LRIP-2 begins. If LRIP-1 is
compressed or its lessons-learned incorporation period is shortened, the
95% target will not be achievable, and the program will enter LRIP-2 with a quality debt that compounds at

6/year.

### 8.2 Quality Prerequisites for Rate Authorization

The following quality conditions are mandatory prerequisites
to LRIP-2 rate authorization above 2 aircraft/year. These conditions are
incorporated as quality sub-criteria within the Gate framework defined

in Section 3.4:

1. **LRIP-1 FTQ sustained at ≥90%** for all
major assembly milestones across the full LRIP-1 lot before LRIP-2

authorization
2. **Non-conformance rate <0.5%** per IMS KPI target

, demonstrated over LRIP-1 production
3. **First Article Inspection (FAI) completed** per
AS9102 for all production processes, not

just the first article
4. **SPC baseline data established** on all
15 critical characteristics planned for SPC deployment in LRIP-2
— SPC control limits require sufficient LRIP-1 data to be stat
istically valid; deploying SPC on inadequate sample sizes renders it ineff

ective precisely when it is most needed
5. **Supplier DPPM <2,000** (
LRIP-1 target) demonstrated at all Tier 1 suppliers before LRIP-2 supply

volumes increase
6. **Retrofit reserve fund at $5M minimum** before LRIP-2

first article completion

### 8.3 Quality Infrastructure Scaling

The 75
QA personnel baseline was sized for the
original production ramp. A 3× throughput increase requires proport
ional QA infrastructure expansion or acceptance of reduced inspection
coverage — the latter is not acceptable given the safety criticality of flight
-critical systems and the autonomous system reliability requirements (≤1×10⁻⁷
per flight hour for catastrophic hazards,
DAL A, C-5 compliance). The program shall:
- Scale QA staffing to maintain the existing inspection coverage ratio

per assembly station and per shift
- Maintain
100% receiving inspection on composite fasteners (first
10 lots per RISK-SC-004)
regardless of throughput increase; staff receiving inspection proportionally
- Expand supplier-site quality engineer presence during the
rate ramp, with a minimum of one quality engineer present at each Tier 1 supplier for the first 90

days of each new rate level

### 8.4 Observation Hold

and Delivery Pipeline Management

The 90-day observation hold on the first 3 production aircraft creates
a delivery pipeline conflict at 6 aircraft/year. With aircraft entering
final assembly every ~61 days, aircraft 4
, 5, and 6 may be in assembly while aircraft

1, 2, and 3 are in observation hold. The program shall:

- Budget for carrying up
to 3 aircraft in completed-but-held inventory status simultaneously during

the LRIP-2 ramp
- Establish an accelerated defect resolution protocol:
any non-conformance discovered during observation hold shall be dispositioned within 14 days, with mandatory retrofit

plan issued before the hold period ends
- Prohibit delivery of any LRIP-2 aircraft
to wildfire operators until the observation hold for that serial number is formally closed and

all findings are disposititioned

### 8.5 Configuration Control at

Accelerated Rate

With 12,500+ configuration items,
8,200+ drawing releases, and 1,200+ quality control points
in the program baseline, the configuration management burden increases mater
ially with 6 aircraft in concurrent production. The Engineering Change Order
(ECO) approval cycle must be validated against 6/year production tempo.
If ECO cycle time exceeds 4 weeks, a fast-track Class
I change process shall be implemented to prevent configuration mis
matches from being built into flight articles. Any
configuration block change that cannot be incorporated into all in-work aircraft within
a 30-day window shall trigger a production hold on the affected work

centers until disposition is complete.

---

## 9. Staffing &

Resource Management

### 9.1 Baseline Workforce Architecture

The Manufacturing Ramp Plan establishes the

following workforce progression:

| Phase | Total Personnel | Increment from

Prior Phase |
|---|---|---|
| LRIP-1 | 150 production |

Baseline |
| LRIP-2 | 280 production | +130 (87% increase) |
| L

RIP-3 | 450 production | +170 |
| FRP-1 | 720 production | +270 |
| F

RP-2 | 1,100 production | +380 |

These figures represent production
personnel only. Quality, engineering support, and program management staff
ing scale proportionally; the 75 QA and 125 engineering support
personnel baselined at peak LRIP must be reassessed against LRIP-2 through

put requirements as described in Section 8.3.

### 9.2 LRIP-1 to

LRIP-2 Workforce Transition Requirements

The LRIP-1 to LRIP-2 transition — adding 130
production personnel (87% headcount increase) — is the shortest-lead workforce
transition in the entire ramp plan. The minimum viable lead time for this

transition is **9 months**, encompassing:

- Recruiting campaign: 2–

3 months
- Candidate screening, background investigation, and offer acceptance: 1–2 months
-

Onboarding and facility access: 2–4 weeks
- Initial quality training (

16 hours mandatory per QMP): 1 week
- Operator qualification
for special processes (composite layup, welding, bonding,
NDT, high-voltage systems): 4–12 weeks depending on process
**The hiring wave for LRIP-2 must be initiated
no later than 9 months before the LRIP-2 target start date.** If the rate
acceleration compresses this window, the program faces a binary choice between del
aying the ramp or deploying undertrained personnel on flight-critical assemblies. The latter

is not acceptable.

The 85,000+ training hours planned for the
production workforce are not discretionary — they include regulatory, air
worthiness, and quality management system requirements that cannot be compressed without
violating the program's own quality management framework and potentially the FAA

certification basis.

### 9.3 High-Voltage and Hybrid
Propulsion Technician Shortage (RISK-M-004

)

This shortage represents the most acute near-term workforce constraint. The specialized EV/
HV technician population required for MAAP-1's hybrid
propulsion integration is limited in the Southern California labor market, and the
6-week internal training program and technical college pipeline

are sized for the baseline ramp. Specific actions required:

- Begin L
RIP-2 EV/HV technician recruitment immediately, regardless of PCB decision on rate change
- Offer signing bonuses and relocation packages to compete
for qualified candidates in a constrained market; budget

$2M–$3M for this accelerated recruitment effort
- Maintain the
1.5:1 technician-to-assembly-station ratio as a

hard constraint — do not authorize station activation without this ratio being met
- Engage
regional technical colleges for an expanded training coh

ort; initiate discussion within 30 days of PCB approval

### 9.4 Cross

-Training and Workforce Resilience Requirements

The FRP transition criteria require
30% of the workforce to be cross-trained before rate increases. At LRIP-1
completion with 150 personnel and limited production history
, this cross-training baseline will not yet exist. The program shall establish
a cross-training tracking metric and target 20% cross-training coverage at LRIP-2
entry as a modified gate criterion, with full 30%

coverage required before LRIP-3 rate increase.

Single-point-of-failure risks
on the production floor — where only one or two workers are qualified on
a given critical process — are not acceptable at any production rate. A
skills matrix shall be maintained and reviewed monthly, with mandatory cross

-training actions for any process where fewer than 3 qualified workers are available.

### 9.

5 Key Personnel Retention

Rate acceleration places disproportionate demands on manufacturing
engineering, quality, and supply chain leadership. The program shall review key personnel retention agreements
for the top 20 positions in these functions and implement
retention incentives before LRIP-2 rate increase is authorized. Loss of key personnel during
an active rate ramp is a program risk with potential Critical-level
consequence; it shall be tracked as an individual risk item

with named owner and succession planning.

### 9.6 Prorated Resource Loading Summary
The following resource loading targets represent the minimum staffing requirements for successful LRIP-2 execution at
6 aircraft/year. These figures require formal update in IMS §

12.3:

| Function | LRIP-1 Baseline | LRIP-2 (

6/year) Required | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Production Personnel | 150 | 280 | +130 |

| Quality Assurance | 35 (LRIP-1 prorated) | 75 | +40 |
| Engineering Support | 75 (

LRIP-1 prorated) | 125 | +50 |
| EV/HV Technicians | 15 | 40 | +25 |

| Supplier Quality Engineers (resident) | 5 | 10 | +5 |
| **Total Program Staff

Delta** | — | — | **+250** |

---

## 10. Overall Assessment &

Recommended Path Forward

### 10.1 Summary Assessment

The proposed production
rate acceleration from 2 to 6 aircraft per year beginning in LRIP-2 is commercially rational
in motivation but operationally premature in timing. The wild
fire fleet demand signal is genuine: CAL FIRE
, Canadian provincial operators, and international firefighting agencies represent
a documented 92-unit base-case
(P50) market through 2035. The program's
MAAP-1F variant is purpose-designed for this mission. The business
case for higher throughput, if executed successfully, is positive
— additional aircraft at a $21.5M unit price
generate ~$86M/year in incremental revenue at the proposed

rate delta.

However, the cross-document analysis uniformly establishes that the program
's manufacturing readiness state, supply chain posture, quality
maturity, workforce availability, and financial reserves at LRIP-2 are
fundamentally incompatible with a 3× rate increase applied without preconditions. On
an FFP contract, every cost overrun from
premature rate acceleration comes directly out of program margin. The probable
cost exposure ($173M–$319M) exceeds the program's entire management
reserve ($74M) by a factor of 2.3× to 4
.3×. The upper bound of this exposure would eliminate program profit entirely
and potentially generate a loss on a program already

carrying a 3% unfavorable cost variance.

Beyond cost
, the quality risk is the most consequential concern. A quality escape
on an early-production MAAP-1F aircraft operating in wildfire conditions — where the aircraft is stressed
to performance limits in smoke, heat, and turbulence — carries catastrophic safety
consequence and would destroy the market opportunity the acceleration is intended to capture, potentially permanently
. The wildfire market is not recoverable after a high-profile loss
-of-aircraft event on an autonomous helicopter in a

media-visible emergency response role.

The program cannot accelerate its
way to market leadership. It must earn it through demonstrated

quality and reliability on the first delivered aircraft.

### 10.2

Decision Recommendation

**The Program Control Board is recommended
to approve the intent of the production rate acceleration while declining to authorize immediate LRIP-2 rate
execution, pending satisfaction of the gated prerequisite framework

defined in Section 3.4.**

This decision posture:
- Preserves the commercial

relationship with wildfire fleet customers by affirming the program's commitment to higher throughput
- Initiates immediate supplier
capacity reservation and notification actions that are required

regardless of final rate decision
- Protects program margin, management reserve, and quality posture by t

ying rate authority to demonstrable readiness
- Maintains the OT&E and
FRP decision sequence integrity required by DoD acquisition policy and FAA certification obligations
- Provides a credible, time-bounded path to 6 aircraft/year that the program can actually

execute successfully

### 10.3 Recommended Actions — Immediate (0–30 Days)

The following actions shall be initiated
immediately upon PM direction, prior to PCB decision, as they

are required regardless of the change outcome:

1. **Initiate CCB review**
of the LRIP-2 rate conflict between the Manufacturing Ramp Plan and IMS. Establish the

authoritative rate baseline before any external communications.
2. **Issue formal capacity inquiry to

GE Aviation** for T700 engine LRIP-2 delivery confirmation.
3. **Issue emergency capacity notice to main transmission supplier (

Supplier X)** and evaluate immediate casting pre-buy orders.
4. **Begin
Workforce Hiring Wave 2 recruitment actions
** for EV/HV technicians and LRIP-2 production personnel. This action
has a 9-month minimum lead time that is already partially consumed.
5. **Commission should-cost modeling** for LRIP-2 at 6 aircraft/year with
bottoms-up validation against supplier NTE quotes

.
6. **Prepare Management Reserve augmentation request** for corporate approval: $50

M–$75M additional reserve to cover rate acceleration risk.
7. **Brief wild
fire fleet customers** that production capacity study is underway; no
delivery commitments are made at accelerated rate pending PCB decision and contract

modification.
8. **Initiate Rate Acceleration Trade Study** per PR
B Section 2 adjudication rules, covering

all five program priorities.

### 10.4 Recommended Actions — Near-Term (30–90 Days)

1
. **Complete PCB review** of this PMP Update;

obtain formal PCB decision on the gated rate increase framework.
2. **Rev
ise all eight affected program documents** per Section 2.

3 within 60 days of PCB approval.
3. **Issue supplier contract modification packages
** to Tier 1 suppliers requiring formal volume commitments at

6/year.
4. **Conduct independent MRL assessment** against SA
E AS2805 criteria at the planned LRIP-2 target

rate; document findings as Gate 7 evidence.
5. **Re-validate
PRB Constraint C-1** ($21
.5M FRP unit price) against revised learning curve model; formally re

-baseline if the model changes.
6. **Establish learning curve tracking dashboard
** with monthly reporting and automatic rate freeze triggers per Section

5.4.
7. **Negotiate MAAP-1F variant contract modification** with wildfire fleet customers for configuration

-specific NRE cost recovery.

### 10.5 Decision Framework

Summary

| Decision Gate | Condition | Authorized Action |
|---|---|---|
|
**Immediate — PCB Pending** | Demand exists
; contracts not yet signed; readiness unconfirmed | Begin supplier

capacity reservation; initiate workforce pipeline; do not authorize tooling investment |
| **
LRIP-1 Complete — Gate Review** | All 8
gate criteria in Section 3.4 satisfied | Authorize rate increase to 3
–4 aircraft/year for LRIP-2 as intermediate step |
| **LRIP-2 Mid-Point — Gate Review** | Learning
curve ≤87% sustained; all quality KPIs met
; firm contracts for 6+ additional aircraft in

hand | Authorize rate increase to 6 aircraft/year for balance of LRIP-2 or LRIP-3 |
| **Any
Point — Rate Freeze Trigger** | Learning curve >88% sustained two
months; NCR >1%; any Gate
criterion failed | Freeze rate at preceding level; mandatory corrective action before

rate restoration |
| **Any Point — Absolute Constraint** | MAAP-1F certification not
closed; OT&E major findings open; MRL <8
| No LRIP-2 deliveries to wildfire operators

regardless of rate authority status |

The correct answer to
this proposed change is not "no." It is "not yet
, not all at once, and not without the readiness that
protects both the program and the customers it is committed to serve." The wildfire fleet demand is
real. The path to meeting it runs through demonstrated quality and manufacturing

maturity — not around them.

# Project Management Plan Update — MAAP-1

**Document Control:** MAAP-1-PMP-UPDATE-001
**Revision:** 1.0
**Classification:** Program Controlled
**Applies To:** All Functional Leads, Program Control Board, Customer Representatives
**Triggered By:** Proposed Change — Production Rate Acceleration, LRIP-2 (2 → 6 Aircraft/Year)
**Authority:** Program Manager, MAAP-1

---

## 1. Introduction

### 1.1 Purpose

This Project Management Plan (PMP) Update documents the programmatic impact, required baseline revisions, risk posture changes, and recommended path forward resulting from the proposed production rate acceleration for the Multi-Mission Autonomous Aircraft Program (MAAP-1). The proposed change — tripling annual production throughput from 2 to 6 aircraft per year beginning in Low-Rate Initial Production Lot 2 (LRIP-2) — constitutes a Class I change under the program's configuration management authority. It affects cost, schedule, technical baseline, supply chain architecture, quality posture, and workforce planning simultaneously, and therefore requires a formal PMP update before any implementation authority is granted.

This document does not authorize the proposed change. It characterizes the change, establishes the analytical basis for the Program Control Board (PCB) decision, and defines the conditional implementation framework if the change is approved.

### 1.2 Background and Change Rationale

The MAAP-1 program is currently executing Engineering and Manufacturing Development (EMD), with Critical Design Review (CDR) at program month (PM) 42, first flight targeted at PM 54, and FAA Type Certification at PM 84. The Low-Rate Initial Production authorization is baselined at PM 72 (WBS 7.8), with LRIP Lot 1 comprising 3 aircraft over 32 weeks and a rate ramp-up activity (WBS 7.8.2) allocated 16 weeks. The Full Rate Production (FRP) Decision is scheduled at PM 84–92, preceded by Operational Test and Evaluation (OT&E) using aircraft PA-1 through PA-6.

The proposed change is commercially motivated. Wildfire fleet demand — principally from CAL FIRE, Canadian provincial operators, and emerging international firefighting agencies — represents a documented market opportunity of 200+ aircraft over fifteen years in the firefighting segment alone, with a base-case (P50) demand of 92 units through 2035 per the Customer Demand Forecast. The MAAP-1F firefighting variant is the primary vehicle for this market. The rate acceleration proposal responds to growing operator urgency and competitive pressure.

However, the manufacturing, supply chain, financial, and quality analyses conducted across all five program documents establish clearly that the program's readiness posture at LRIP-2 is fundamentally incompatible with a 3× rate increase applied without preconditions. Manufacturing readiness is insufficient, supply chain contracts are sized for baseline throughput, learning curve benefits have not been captured, OT&E and FRP decision gates remain open, and the MAAP-1F FAA certification basis for autonomous firefighting operations has not been closed. On a Firm Fixed Price (FFP) contract structure, cost growth from premature rate acceleration is absorbed entirely by program margin.

A critical internal document inconsistency must also be acknowledged at the outset. The Manufacturing Ramp Plan already specifies 6 aircraft per year as the LRIP-2 baseline rate, planned for 2027, with the LRIP-1 rate established at 2 aircraft per year for 2025–2026. The Supply Chain Procurement Plan similarly reflects 12 engines (6 aircraft) for LRIP-2. However, the IMS baseline documents LRIP production at approximately 1 aircraft per 10 weeks (~5.2/year) with LRIP Lot 1 at 3 aircraft over 32 weeks, and several risk register entries are sized against a lower baseline demand. This inconsistency must be formally reconciled by the Configuration Control Board (CCB) before any LRIP-2 rate decision is made. The impact assessments in this PMP Update proceed on the interpretation most consistent with the greatest risk exposure: that the proposed change represents either (a) a compression or elimination of the LRIP-1 stabilization period, or (b) an acceleration of the LRIP-2 start date beyond what the program's current readiness state supports, regardless of whether 6/year was always the planned LRIP-2 target.

### 1.3 Document Scope and Authority

This PMP Update applies to all program functional areas. It supersedes conflicting guidance in the program baseline for topics addressed herein and is effective upon Program Manager signature. Implementation of any production rate change described in this document requires separate PCB approval with documented exit criteria satisfaction.

## 2. Project Scope

### 2.1 Change Scope Definition

The proposed change modifies the following program scope elements:

**Production Rate:** LRIP-2 annual throughput increases from 2 to 6 aircraft per year, a 3× step increase applied before OT&E completion, before the FRP decision gate, and before MAAP-1F FAA certification is closed for autonomous firefighting operations.

**Manufacturing Scope:** Achieving 6 aircraft per year requires activation of a second Final Assembly Line (FAL) flow, installation and qualification of a second autoclave (currently approved but undelivered), potential addition of a third assembly station (currently a contingency, not a baseline), expanded tooling and fixtures, and two-shift operations. All of these actions must transition from contingency or FRP-phase plans to LRIP-phase commitments.

**Supply Chain Scope:** Long-term agreements with Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers must be renegotiated to reflect 6/year volume. Current agreements were sized for 2/year LRIP throughput. The 200% rate increase exceeds existing contractual rate-increase options (capped at 50% with 12-month notice) for most suppliers, necessitating formal contract modifications. Supplier capacity expansion investments — including propulsion system suppliers requiring a 24-month expansion timeline — must be funded and underway before LRIP-2 rate commitment.

**Workforce Scope:** The production workforce must grow from the 150-person LRIP-1 complement to 280 persons (Workforce Hiring Wave 2) at minimum, with the hiring wave initiated 9 months before LRIP-2 start. Specialized high-voltage and hybrid propulsion technician shortfalls (RISK-M-004) must be resolved before rate increase. The 85,000+ training hours planned for the production workforce cannot be compressed without quality consequences.

**Quality Scope:** The Quality Management Plan's LRIP-1 first-time quality (FTQ) target of 90% must be achieved and validated before LRIP-2 rate increase. At 6 aircraft per year, a 90% FTQ rate generates rework burden on 10% of assemblies at three times the baseline volume, creating a rework cascade incompatible with delivery commitments on an immature line. The 90-day observation hold on first production aircraft creates delivery pipeline conflicts at the accelerated rate.

**Financial Scope:** The $74M management reserve (1.5% of contract value), sized for the baseline ramp, is insufficient to absorb the cost exposure created by rate acceleration. Capital expenditure for LRIP-2 preparation ($52M in the Manufacturing Ramp Plan baseline) may need to be front-loaded into earlier periods, and total capital commitment could reach $120M in a single year, competing with the $68M already planned for LRIP-1 activation.

**Certification Scope:** MAAP-1F firefighting variant aircraft shall not be delivered to wildfire operators in quantities that exceed demonstrated FAA regulatory authorization for the intended mission profile. LRIP-2 aircraft produced before the MAAP-1F certification basis is closed carry retrofit exposure if any post-LRIP-2 certification finding requires design modification.

### 2.2 Scope Exclusions

This PMP Update does not authorize:
- Any increase in total program aircraft quantity beyond the currently contracted baseline
- FRP-phase rate increases beyond the 24 aircraft/year FRP target
- Changes to the MAAP-1E or MAAP-1R variant production schedules
- Relaxation of DAL A/B software qualification requirements, DO-178C compliance, or safety-critical system acceptance test coverage at any production rate

| Production Process Validation Complete | WBS 7.7.1–7.7.4 | November 2033 |
| Production Readiness Review | WBS 7.7.4 | November 2033 |
| LRIP Authorization | WBS 7.8 | 16-Nov-33 |
| LRIP Lot 1 (3 aircraft, 32 weeks) | WBS 7.8.1 | Nov 2033 – Jul 2034 |
| Rate Ramp-Up Activity | WBS 7.8.2 | 16 weeks concurrent |
| IOC | WBS 7.9.4 | 25-Jul-34 |
| OT&E Period (PA-1 through PA-6) | TEMP Section 12.1 | PM 72–84 |

### 3.2 Schedule Impact of Proposed Change

Adding LRIP-2 at 6 aircraft per year before the FRP decision introduces new critical path activities with durations of 12–40 weeks that are not currently scheduled. The following new activities become hard prerequisites to LRIP-2 rate authorization, each with fixed minimum durations driven by physical and contractual constraints:

| Prerequisite Activity | Minimum Lead Before LRIP-2 | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| Phase 1 facility expansion | 18 months | Construction — not schedule-elastic |
| Second autoclave delivery and qualification | 14–18 months | Capital equipment procurement |
| Additional tooling order (airframe, composite, FAL) | 14 months (48-week fabrication + 12-week qualification) | Hard lead time |
| Workforce Hiring Wave 2 (LRIP-1 → LRIP-2 increment) | 9 months minimum | Recruiting, onboarding, qualification |
| Supplier contract modifications (engine, transmission, avionics) | 12 months (rate-increase notice requirement) | Contractual |
| MRL-8 achievement confirmation | Gate — must precede rate authority | Regulatory/program governance |
| LRIP-1 composite layup yield ≥82% sustained for 3 months | Gate — must precede rate authority | Quality prerequisite |

A schedule risk assessment places the aggregate slip potential from these prerequisite gaps at 4–12 months on IOC and the FRP Decision Review, if the rate increase is attempted without satisfying the prerequisites. An IOC slip of this magnitude would push IOC from July 2034 to the November 2034 – July 2035 window, with corresponding FRP delay — directly undermining the market capture rationale for acceleration.

### 3.3 Required IMS Revisions

The following IMS modifications are required before LRIP-2 rate authority can be formally considered:

**New WBS Element — 7.8.4, LRIP Lot 2:** Define scope, aircraft quantity, duration, start/finish dates, and all predecessor/successor dependencies. Integrate into critical path analysis with zero-float tracking restored.

**Revised WBS 7.8.2 (Rate Ramp-Up):** Extend duration and resequence to reflect the higher target rate. The current 16-week allocation is insufficient for a 3× ramp.

**Revised §12.3 Resource Loading:** Update peak manufacturing staff from 450 to reflect accelerated hiring, QA personnel from 75 to reflect proportional scaling, and total manufacturing hours from 3.2M to reflect 6/year throughput.

**Critical Path Re-run:** All additions to the LRIP phase must be re-evaluated for IOC (WBS 7.9.4) and FRP Decision (WBS 7.10.3) cascade impact. Schedule acceleration opportunities elsewhere in the network must be identified to preserve the IOC date, or IOC/FRP dates must be formally re-baselined through PCB.

### 3.4 Proposed Rate Gate Milestone Framework

This PMP Update establishes a gated rate increase framework as the authorized implementation mechanism if the proposed change is approved by PCB. No LRIP-2 rate authority above 2 aircraft/year is granted until all gates below are passed and documented. Gates are evaluated sequentially; failure of any gate holds the rate at the preceding level.

| Gate | Criterion | Target Validation Point |
|---|---|---|
| Gate 1 — Autoclave Readiness | Second autoclave operational, qualified, and producing conforming composite parts for 30 days continuous | Before LRIP-2 composite demand peaks |
| Gate 2 — Composite Yield | Composite layup first-pass yield ≥82% sustained over 3 consecutive months | After aircraft #3 delivery (LRIP-1) |
| Gate 3 — Supply Chain Contracts | All Tier 1 and critical Tier 2 suppliers formally contracted at 6/year volumes; capacity confirmed in writing | 6 months before LRIP-2 start |
| Gate 4 — FAL Third Station | Third assembly station installed, flow-balanced, and flow time validated at ≤10 weeks per aircraft | Before LRIP-2 authorization |
| Gate 5 — Workforce | EV/HV technician headcount at 1.5:1 ratio for all LRIP-2 stations; all personnel training-complete | Before LRIP-2 workforce activation |
| Gate 6 — Quality Reserve | Retrofit reserve fund increased from $2M to minimum $5M; QA staffing plan validated for 6/year throughput | Before LRIP-2 authorization |
| Gate 7 — MRL Assessment | Independent Manufacturing Readiness Level assessment confirms MRL ≥ 8 against SAE AS2805 criteria at 6/year rate | At LRIP-1 exit |
| Gate 8 — MAAP-1F Certification | FAA certification basis closed for firefighting variant; no open certification findings requiring design action | Before LRIP-2 aircraft delivered to wildfire customers |

## 4. Change Management Plan

### 4.1 Change Classification

The proposed production rate acceleration meets the threshold for a **Class I Engineering Change** under the program's Configuration Management authority (WBS 8.2.4). The classification basis is:

- **Schedule Impact:** Greater than 30 days on multiple IMS milestones; potential IOC slip of 4–12 months
- **Cost Impact:** Estimated incremental cost exposure of $91M–$199M (probable range), with upper-bound scenarios approaching $319M
- **Contract Scope:** Rate increase of 200% exceeds existing contractual rate-increase options (capped at 50%) with most Tier 1 suppliers, requiring contract modifications
- **Technical Baseline:** Tooling, facility, and staffing changes required; make/buy decisions require re-adjudication; configuration management burden increases with 6 aircraft in concurrent production
- **Customer Impact:** Delivery schedule changes to wildfire fleet customers; potential for delivery of aircraft ahead of contracted demand; MAAP-1F certification dependency

### 4.2 Change Control Board Process

1. **Program Manager** submits formal Change Request with this PMP Update as the supporting analysis document
2. **CCB Review** — minimum attendees: PM, Chief Engineer, Manufacturing Director, Supply Chain Director, Chief Financial Officer, Quality Director, Contracts Lead, Customer Representative
3. **PCB Decision** — approve, approve with conditions (gated framework per Section 3.4), or reject
4. **Baseline Revision** — if approved, all eight documents identified in Section 2.3 are formally revised per the Document Control process within 60 days of PCB approval
5. **Customer Notification** — Contract modification negotiations initiated with wildfire fleet customers within 30 days of PCB approval; no delivery commitments made at accelerated rate until contract modifications are executed

### 4.3 Change Communication Plan

| Audience | Message | Timing | Channel |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program Control Board | Full PMP Update — risk, cost, schedule, quality implications | Immediate | Formal PCB package |
| Manufacturing Director | Gate criteria, tooling and facility investment requirements | Within 7 days | Functional lead briefing |
| Supply Chain Director | Immediate supplier notification actions (Section 7) | Within 7 days | Functional lead briefing |
| Customer (CAL FIRE, etc.) | Production capacity study underway; no commitment until PCB approval and contract modification | Within 14 days | Program Manager letter |
| Corporate Finance | Reserve augmentation request; capital expenditure front-loading implications | Within 14 days | EAC revision brief |
| FAA (as applicable) | No regulatory communication until MAAP-1F certification path is confirmed | As determined by Legal/Contracts | Formal channel |

### 4.4 Conditions Applicable to All Rate Increase Decisions

Regardless of PCB decision, the following conditions apply to any production rate increase in the LRIP phase:

- **Demand must be contractually secured before rate increase is authorized.** Market demand forecasts and letters of intent do not constitute sufficient basis for rate authority. Firm, funded customer contracts must be in hand, with milestone payment terms that recover the cost of acceleration.
- **OT&E findings must be assessed before LRIP-2 deliveries to operators.** Aircraft delivered before OT&E closes carry retrofit risk if findings require design correction. The program shall maintain a retrofit reserve and design freeze protocol consistent with LRIP-2 delivery commitments.
- **The MAAP-1F certification-gated delivery constraint (Gate 8) is non-negotiable.** Delivering aircraft to firefighting operators before the FAA certification basis is closed for autonomous operations creates regulatory and safety exposure that the program cannot accept. This constraint is treated as equivalent to a program safety requirement.

## 5. Cost Management Plan & Cost Baseline

### 5.1 Current Financial Baseline

The program's approved financial baseline establishes:

- **Total Program Cost:** $4,380M
- **Total Program Revenue:** $4,778M
- **Gross Margin (Baseline):** $398M (9.1%)
- **Management Reserve:** $74M (1.5% of contract value)
- **FRP Unit Price Constraint (PRB C-1):** ≤$21.5M per aircraft at FRP Lot 5 (FY25$)
- **Current EVM Status:** 3% unfavorable cost variance (RISK-C-001, rated Critical, score 16) — the program is already carrying negative cost performance before the proposed change is implemented
- **Learning Curve Assumption:** 85% (conservative vs. industry 82%)
- **LRIP-2 Unit Cost Reduction Target:** 12% from LRIP-1 average

### 5.2 Cost Impact of Proposed Change

The proposed rate acceleration creates cost growth across six primary drivers. The ranges below represent probable (not worst-case) outcomes based on cross-document financial analysis:

| Cost Driver | Estimated Impact | Confidence | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Facility and tooling acceleration (pull-forward of FRP-phase capital) | +$50M – $80M | Medium | Manufacturing Ramp Plan; IMS Section 10.4 |
| Supply chain expedite premiums and contract renegotiation | +$25M – $45M | Medium-High | RISK-SC-002 spot-market premium (15%); engine expedite scenario ($48M) |
| Learning curve degradation (90% actual vs. 85% plan) | +$60M – $125M | Medium | Financial model scenario; pro-rated to LRIP impact |
| Overtime, shift premium, and surge labor costs | +$15M – $25M | High | RISK-M-004 technician premium; two-shift contingency becoming baseline |
| Composite structures BUY penalty (delayed make transition) | +$3M – $4M per year at 6 units | High | $565K/aircraft cost differential (BOM: $1,850K buy vs. $1,285K internal) |
| Quality escapes, rework, scrap, and retrofit reserve shortfall | +$20M – $40M | Medium | RISK-M-005; $2M reserve undersized at 3× volume |
| **Total Probable Incremental Cost Exposure** | **+$173M – $319M** | | |

The midpoint of this range (~$245M) represents approximately 5.6% of the $4,380M program cost baseline. Applied against the baseline $398M gross margin, the midpoint exposure alone reduces gross margin from 9.1% to approximately 4.1% — below most corporate hurdle rates and well below program target. At the upper bound (+$319M), the program approaches breakeven or loss territory, consistent with the modeled "Perfect Storm" scenario outcome (-1.2% gross margin).

### 5.3 Cost Constraint C-1 Reassessment

The PRB Constraint C-1 (≤$21.5M/aircraft at FRP Lot 5) was premised on a learning curve derived from the baseline production ramp. Accelerating LRIP-2 before manufacturing processes are stable may improve the FRP cost trajectory if executed cleanly — the unit cost reduction from volume production is real. However, if the ramp outpaces readiness (the high-probability scenario given the identified gaps), increased rework, scrap, overtime, and expedite costs in LRIP-2 will flatten the learning curve and erode the cost baseline from which FRP pricing is projected. A formal re-validation of C-1 against a revised learning curve model reflecting 6/year LRIP-2 production must be completed before PCB approval of the rate increase.

### 5.4 Learning Curve Corrective Action Protocol

A formal learning curve tracking dashboard shall be established with monthly reporting. The following protocol applies regardless of PCB decision on the rate change:

- **Trigger Level 1 (Watch):** Actual learning curve exceeds 87% for two consecutive months → Program Manager notification; root cause analysis initiated
- **Trigger Level 2 (Corrective Action):** Actual learning curve exceeds 87% for three consecutive months → Rate increase suspended pending root cause and recovery plan; mandatory PCB briefing
- **Trigger Level 3 (Rate Reduction):** Actual learning curve exceeds 90% for any month → Production rate reduced to preceding rate level; independent assessment required before rate is restored

### 5.5 Management Reserve Augmentation

The existing $74M management reserve is structurally insufficient to absorb the midpoint cost exposure ($245M) identified above. If the PCB approves the rate acceleration, the program shall:

1. Request corporate approval for **$50M – $75M additional management reserve** specifically designated for rate acceleration risk, bringing total reserve to $124M – $149M (approximately 2.5–3.0% of contract value)
2. Frame this reserve augmentation as investment protection for the acceleration decision, not as acknowledgment of program overrun
3. Maintain the reserve in a separately tracked cost account with draw-down authorization requiring PM and CFO co-signature

### 5.6 Cash Flow Implications

The current cumulative cash trough is -$103M (2029). Rate acceleration front-loads costs before deliveries generate milestone payments, deepening this trough. Key cash flow actions:

- **Progress payment rate review:** Evaluate requesting increase from 80% to 85% during the ramp surge period to partially offset the cash flow impact of accelerated cost absorption
- **Milestone payment acceleration:** Negotiate with wildfire fleet customers for earlier milestone payment triggers (e.g., delivery acceptance at factory rather than customer site acceptance) to improve cash timing
- **Capital expenditure staging:** Do not commit to the full $52M LRIP-2 preparation investment in a single fiscal year; stage against gate criteria in Section 3.4 to preserve capital optionality

### 5.7 Should-Cost Modeling Requirement

Before committing to the rate increase, the program shall complete bottoms-up should-cost modeling for LRIP-2 and LRIP-3 at 6 aircraft per year, validated against supplier quotes with not-to-exceed (NTE) caps. Parametric estimates alone are insufficient authorization basis for an FFP commitment of this scope.

- **Probability:** High | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 16 (Critical)**
- **Owner:** Chief Engineer / Test Director
- **Mitigation:** Maintain design freeze protocol; retrofit reserve at $5M minimum; do not deliver LRIP-2 aircraft to operators until OT&E major findings are dispositioned

- **Description:** Producing 6 MAAP-1F aircraft per year before FAA authorization for autonomous firefighting operations is obtained leaves completed aircraft with no legal delivery pathway, generating inventory carrying costs and potential retrofit requirements for any certification-driven design changes
- **Probability:** Medium | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 12 (High)**

- **Description:** The IMS baseline carries zero critical path float. New prerequisite activities for rate acceleration (facility expansion, tooling, autoclave qualification) add parallel-path pressures with fixed durations, consuming all remaining schedule margin and extending IOC and FRP dates
- **Probability:** High | **Consequence:** High | **Score: 12 (High)**
- **Owner:** Program Manager / Scheduler
- **Mitigation:** Do not begin rate acceleration activities without dedicated resources that are additions to, not substitutions from, existing critical path resources; re-run critical path analysis immediately
