## 7. Procurement / Supply Chain Management Plan

### 7.1 Immediate Supplier Actions (0–30 Days)

The following actions are required immediately, regardless of PCB decision on the rate change. These are information-gathering and contractual notice actions that do not commit the program to the rate increase but preserve optionality and satisfy notice period obligations:

1. **Reconcile internal document conflict.** The Supply Chain Procurement Plan reflects 12 engines (6 aircraft) for LRIP-2, while other program documents reflect lower LRIP-2 demand. A CCB-reviewed reconciliation must establish the authoritative baseline before any supplier notification is issued.

2. **Issue formal capacity inquiry to GE Aviation (T700 engines, 52+ week lead time).** Confirm whether LRIP-2 quantity of 12 engines is already under contract and whether delivery cadence aligns to any proposed accelerated start date. The program's contractual rate-increase option allows 50% quantity increase with 12-month notice — a 200% increase requires contract modification and must be initiated immediately.

3. **Issue emergency capacity review notice to main transmission supplier (Supplier X, 40-week casting lead time).** The transmission is a sole-source, clean-sheet design with no buffer inventory planned until LRIP-3. Supplier X is still in the NRE/qualification phase. Immediate casting pre-buy orders for a 6-month buffer must be evaluated and, if justified, placed pending PCB rate decision.

4. **Survey all Tier 1 and critical Tier 2 suppliers** against a 6/year demand signal. Require written capacity confirmation or identification of expansion investment and timeline required to support 6/year. This survey provides the evidence base for Gate 3 in the rate gate framework.

5. **Issue 12-month rate increase notifications** to all suppliers where 6/year LRIP-2 is not already contracted. This preserves the contractual notice clock regardless of PCB decision and avoids further erosion of the available lead time.

### 7.2 Long-Lead Item Procurement Strategy

Long-lead items governing LRIP-2 production must have purchase orders in the pipeline before assembly demand materializes. At a 52-week maximum lead time, engines for the 6th LRIP-2 aircraft must be on order before the first LRIP-1 aircraft is delivered. The following schedule governs acceptable procurement action timing:

| Item | Lead Time | Required PO Date for 6/Year LRIP-2 | Current PO Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| GE T700 Engine Assembly | 52+ weeks | 12 months before each assembly start | Confirm immediately |
| Main Transmission Housing (castings) | 40 weeks | 10 months before each assembly start | Issue casting orders immediately |
| AESA Radar System | 48 weeks | 12 months before avionics installation | Confirm/place |
| Main Landing Gear Forging | 40 weeks | 10 months before landing gear installation | Pre-buy revision required |
| Ejection Seat Assembly | 44 weeks | 11 months before seat installation | Confirm capacity |
| Flight Control Computer | 40 weeks | 10 months before avionics installation | Verify against 60-unit order coverage |
| APU (Honeywell GTCP36-150) | 36 weeks | 9 months before installation | Standard scheduling |

### 7.3 Contract Strategy Revisions

**Rate-Increase Options Ceiling:** Existing supplier contracts cap rate-increase options at 50% with 12-month advance notice. The proposed 200% rate increase (2 to 6 aircraft/year) universally exceeds this ceiling for suppliers contracted at the 2/year baseline. Formal contract modifications, not option exercises, are required. Budget negotiation authority must be allocated for potential pricing premium concessions required to secure supplier agreement to accelerated volumes.

**Multi-Year Fixed-Price Commitment Strategy:** The program's existing multi-year fixed-price contract strategy (exemplified by the $185M rotor blade set commitment) is the correct model to replicate across the accelerated supply base. Long-term fixed-price commitments at 6/year volumes provide cost certainty, lock in pricing before demand signal drives spot-market premiums, and give suppliers the revenue confidence to fund capacity expansion investments.

**MAAP-1F Variant Supply Chain:** The firefighting variant includes mission-specific equipment (retardant delivery systems, firefighting sensors, terrain mapping) not in the baseline BOM. These represent Class I change exposure of $20M–$50M in engineering and qualification costs. If not captured in a contract modification with the wildfire fleet customer, they erode margin directly. A separate contract line item or modification for MAAP-1F variant NRE and qualification costs must be negotiated before LRIP-2 firefighting-variant production is authorized.

### 7.4 Make/Buy Re-Adjudication

The make/buy strategy established in the Manufacturing Ramp Plan and BOM must be formally re-adjudicated for all items currently designated MAKE against available internal capacity at 6 aircraft/year. Key findings from initial analysis:

- **Composite fuselage barrels:** In-sourcing trigger remains at 12+ units/year (LRIP-3). At 6/year, continue buying at $1,850K/set. No make/buy change triggered.
- **CNC machined structures:** In-sourcing trigger at >50 units/year total. Continue buying at 6/year. No change.
- **Wiring harnesses:** In-house from LRIP-1. Capacity validation for 6/year required — internal capacity audit mandated before LRIP-2.
- **Final assembly:** In-house at all phases; second assembly flow activation is the critical LRIP-2 production action.

### 7.5 Supply Chain Risk Monitoring

A **weekly supplier quality review** shall be added to the program management cadence during the LRIP-1/LRIP-2 transition period, supplementing the existing weekly kit shortage meeting. A **Rate Readiness Index** metric shall be developed and reported monthly at PCB, tracking each Tier 1 supplier's demonstrated capacity versus committed 6/year demand.

Any new supplier qualifications required by rate acceleration must include full trusted supplier program assessment per NIST 800-161, maintaining the program's supply chain infiltration risk controls even under schedule pressure.

## 8. Quality Management Plan

### 8.1 Quality Posture at Baseline vs. Accelerated Rate

The Quality Management Plan establishes a deliberate quality maturation staircase premised on process stabilization at each rate before advancement to the next:

| Phase | FTQ Target | Cpk Target | Supplier DPPM | Scrap/Rework |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LRIP-1 | 90% | ≥1.33 | <2,000 | Baseline |
| LRIP-2 | 95% | ≥1.33 | <1,000 | <3% |
| LRIP-3 | 97% | ≥1.50 | <500 | <2% |
| FRP-1 | 98% | ≥1.67 | <500 | <1% |

The LRIP-1 FTQ target of 90% is intentional — it represents the expected quality level during process learning, not a failure. The problem created by rate acceleration is volume: at 90% FTQ and 6 aircraft/year, 10% of major assemblies require rework or rejection across three times the production volume, generating a rework backlog that cascades through the FAL schedule and consumes capacity that should be going to productive assembly.

Achieving the LRIP-2 FTQ target of 95% requires that LRIP-1 quality lessons are fully incorporated before LRIP-2 begins. If LRIP-1 is compressed or its lessons-learned incorporation period is shortened, the 95% target will not be achievable, and the program will enter LRIP-2 with a quality debt that compounds at 6/year.

### 8.2 Quality Prerequisites for Rate Authorization

The following quality conditions are mandatory prerequisites to LRIP-2 rate authorization above 2 aircraft/year. These conditions are incorporated as quality sub-criteria within the Gate framework defined in Section 3.4:

1. **LRIP-1 FTQ sustained at ≥90%** for all major assembly milestones across the full LRIP-1 lot before LRIP-2 authorization
2. **Non-conformance rate <0.5%** per IMS KPI target, demonstrated over LRIP-1 production
3. **First Article Inspection (FAI) completed** per AS9102 for all production processes, not just the first article
4. **SPC baseline data established** on all 15 critical characteristics planned for SPC deployment in LRIP-2 — SPC control limits require sufficient LRIP-1 data to be statistically valid; deploying SPC on inadequate sample sizes renders it ineffective precisely when it is most needed
5. **Supplier DPPM <2,000** (LRIP-1 target) demonstrated at all Tier 1 suppliers before LRIP-2 supply volumes increase
6. **Retrofit reserve fund at $5M minimum** before LRIP-2 first article completion

### 8.3 Quality Infrastructure Scaling

The 75 QA personnel baseline was sized for the original production ramp. A 3× throughput increase requires proportional QA infrastructure expansion or acceptance of reduced inspection coverage — the latter is not acceptable given the safety criticality of flight-critical systems and the autonomous system reliability requirements (≤1×10⁻⁷ per flight hour for catastrophic hazards, DAL A, C-5 compliance). The program shall:

- Scale QA staffing to maintain the existing inspection coverage ratio per assembly station and per shift
- Maintain 100% receiving inspection on composite fasteners (first 10 lots per RISK-SC-004) regardless of throughput increase; staff receiving inspection proportionally
- Expand supplier-site quality engineer presence during the rate ramp, with a minimum of one quality engineer present at each Tier 1 supplier for the first 90 days of each new rate level

### 8.4 Observation Hold and Delivery Pipeline Management

The 90-day observation hold on the first 3 production aircraft creates a delivery pipeline conflict at 6 aircraft/year. With aircraft entering final assembly every ~61 days, aircraft 4, 5, and 6 may be in assembly while aircraft 1, 2, and 3 are in observation hold. The program shall:

- Budget for carrying up to 3 aircraft in completed-but-held inventory status simultaneously during the LRIP-2 ramp
- Establish an accelerated defect resolution protocol: any non-conformance discovered during observation hold shall be dispositioned within 14 days, with mandatory retrofit plan issued before the hold period ends
- Prohibit delivery of any LRIP-2 aircraft to wildfire operators until the observation hold for that serial number is formally closed and all findings are disposititioned

### 8.5 Configuration Control at Accelerated Rate

With 12,500+ configuration items, 8,200+ drawing releases, and 1,200+ quality control points in the program baseline, the configuration management burden increases materially with 6 aircraft in concurrent production. The Engineering Change Order (ECO) approval cycle must be validated against 6/year production tempo. If ECO cycle time exceeds 4 weeks, a fast-track Class I change process shall be implemented to prevent configuration mismatches from being built into flight articles. Any configuration block change that cannot be incorporated into all in-work aircraft within a 30-day window shall trigger a production hold on the affected work centers until disposition is complete.

## 9. Staffing & Resource Management

### 9.1 Baseline Workforce Architecture

The Manufacturing Ramp Plan establishes the following workforce progression:

| Phase | Total Personnel | Increment from Prior Phase |
|---|---|---|
| LRIP-1 | 150 production | Baseline |
| LRIP-2 | 280 production | +130 (87% increase) |
| LRIP-3 | 450 production | +170 |
| FRP-1 | 720 production | +270 |
| FRP-2 | 1,100 production | +380 |

These figures represent production personnel only. Quality, engineering support, and program management staffing scale proportionally; the 75 QA and 125 engineering support personnel baselined at peak LRIP must be reassessed against LRIP-2 throughput requirements as described in Section 8.3.

### 9.2 LRIP-1 to LRIP-2 Workforce Transition Requirements

The LRIP-1 to LRIP-2 transition — adding 130 production personnel (87% headcount increase) — is the shortest-lead workforce transition in the entire ramp plan. The minimum viable lead time for this transition is **9 months**, encompassing:

- Recruiting campaign: 2–3 months
- Candidate screening, background investigation, and offer acceptance: 1–2 months
- Onboarding and facility access: 2–4 weeks
- Initial quality training (16 hours mandatory per QMP): 1 week
- Operator qualification for special processes (composite layup, welding, bonding, NDT, high-voltage systems): 4–12 weeks depending on process

**The hiring wave for LRIP-2 must be initiated no later than 9 months before the LRIP-2 target start date.** If the rate acceleration compresses this window, the program faces a binary choice between delaying the ramp or deploying undertrained personnel on flight-critical assemblies. The latter is not acceptable.

The 85,000+ training hours planned for the production workforce are not discretionary — they include regulatory, airworthiness, and quality management system requirements that cannot be compressed without violating the program's own quality management framework and potentially the FAA certification basis.

### 9.3 High-Voltage and Hybrid Propulsion Technician Shortage (RISK-M-004)

This shortage represents the most acute near-term workforce constraint. The specialized EV/HV technician population required for MAAP-1's hybrid propulsion integration is limited in the Southern California labor market, and the 6-week internal training program and technical college pipeline are sized for the baseline ramp. Specific actions required:

- Begin LRIP-2 EV/HV technician recruitment immediately, regardless of PCB decision on rate change
- Offer signing bonuses and relocation packages to compete for qualified candidates in a constrained market; budget $2M–$3M for this accelerated recruitment effort
- Maintain the 1.5:1 technician-to-assembly-station ratio as a hard constraint — do not authorize station activation without this ratio being met
- Engage regional technical colleges for an expanded training cohort; initiate discussion within 30 days of PCB approval

### 9.4 Cross-Training and Workforce Resilience Requirements

The FRP transition criteria require 30% of the workforce to be cross-trained before rate increases. At LRIP-1 completion with 150 personnel and limited production history, this cross-training baseline will not yet exist. The program shall establish a cross-training tracking metric and target 20% cross-training coverage at LRIP-2 entry as a modified gate criterion, with full 30% coverage required before LRIP-3 rate increase.

Single-point-of-failure risks on the production floor — where only one or two workers are qualified on a given critical process — are not acceptable at any production rate. A skills matrix shall be maintained and reviewed monthly, with mandatory cross-training actions for any process where fewer than 3 qualified workers are available.

### 9.5 Key Personnel Retention

Rate acceleration places disproportionate demands on manufacturing engineering, quality, and supply chain leadership. The program shall review key personnel retention agreements for the top 20 positions in these functions and implement retention incentives before LRIP-2 rate increase is authorized. Loss of key personnel during an active rate ramp is a program risk with potential Critical-level consequence; it shall be tracked as an individual risk item with named owner and succession planning.

### 9.6 Prorated Resource Loading Summary

The following resource loading targets represent the minimum staffing requirements for successful LRIP-2 execution at 6 aircraft/year. These figures require formal update in IMS §12.3:

| Function | LRIP-1 Baseline | LRIP-2 (6/year) Required | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Production Personnel | 150 | 280 | +130 |
| Quality Assurance | 35 (LRIP-1 prorated) | 75 | +40 |
| Engineering Support | 75 (LRIP-1 prorated) | 125 | +50 |
| EV/HV Technicians | 15 | 40 | +25 |
| Supplier Quality Engineers (resident) | 5 | 10 | +5 |
| **Total Program Staff Delta** | — | — | **+250** |

## 10. Overall Assessment & Recommended Path Forward

### 10.1 Summary Assessment

The proposed production rate acceleration from 2 to 6 aircraft per year beginning in LRIP-2 is commercially rational in motivation but operationally premature in timing. The wildfire fleet demand signal is genuine: CAL FIRE, Canadian provincial operators, and international firefighting agencies represent a documented 92-unit base-case (P50) market through 2035. The program's MAAP-1F variant is purpose-designed for this mission. The business case for higher throughput, if executed successfully, is positive — additional aircraft at a $21.5M unit price generate ~$86M/year in incremental revenue at the proposed rate delta.

However, the cross-document analysis uniformly establishes that the program's manufacturing readiness state, supply chain posture, quality maturity, workforce availability, and financial reserves at LRIP-2 are fundamentally incompatible with a 3× rate increase applied without preconditions. On an FFP contract, every cost overrun from premature rate acceleration comes directly out of program margin. The probable cost exposure ($173M–$319M) exceeds the program's entire management reserve ($74M) by a factor of 2.3× to 4.3×. The upper bound of this exposure would eliminate program profit entirely and potentially generate a loss on a program already carrying a 3% unfavorable cost variance.

Beyond cost, the quality risk is the most consequential concern. A quality escape on an early-production MAAP-1F aircraft operating in wildfire conditions — where the aircraft is stressed to performance limits in smoke, heat, and turbulence — carries catastrophic safety consequence and would destroy the market opportunity the acceleration is intended to capture, potentially permanently. The wildfire market is not recoverable after a high-profile loss-of-aircraft event on an autonomous helicopter in a media-visible emergency response role.

The program cannot accelerate its way to market leadership. It must earn it through demonstrated quality and reliability on the first delivered aircraft.

### 10.2 Decision Recommendation

**The Program Control Board is recommended to approve the intent of the production rate acceleration while declining to authorize immediate LRIP-2 rate execution, pending satisfaction of the gated prerequisite framework defined in Section 3.4.**

This decision posture:
- Preserves the commercial relationship with wildfire fleet customers by affirming the program's commitment to higher throughput
- Initiates immediate supplier capacity reservation and notification actions that are required regardless of final rate decision
- Protects program margin, management reserve, and quality posture by tying rate authority to demonstrable readiness
- Maintains the OT&E and FRP decision sequence integrity required by DoD acquisition policy and FAA certification obligations
- Provides a credible, time-bounded path to 6 aircraft/year that the program can actually execute successfully

### 10.3 Recommended Actions — Immediate (0–30 Days)

The following actions shall be initiated immediately upon PM direction, prior to PCB decision, as they are required regardless of the change outcome:

1. **Initiate CCB review** of the LRIP-2 rate conflict between the Manufacturing Ramp Plan and IMS. Establish the authoritative rate baseline before any external communications.
2. **Issue formal capacity inquiry to GE Aviation** for T700 engine LRIP-2 delivery confirmation.
3. **Issue emergency capacity notice to main transmission supplier (Supplier X)** and evaluate immediate casting pre-buy orders.
4. **Begin Workforce Hiring Wave 2 recruitment actions** for EV/HV technicians and LRIP-2 production personnel. This action has a 9-month minimum lead time that is already partially consumed.
5. **Commission should-cost modeling** for LRIP-2 at 6 aircraft/year with bottoms-up validation against supplier NTE quotes.
6. **Prepare Management Reserve augmentation request** for corporate approval: $50M–$75M additional reserve to cover rate acceleration risk.
7. **Brief wildfire fleet customers** that production capacity study is underway; no delivery commitments are made at accelerated rate pending PCB decision and contract modification.
8. **Initiate Rate Acceleration Trade Study** per PRB Section 2 adjudication rules, covering all five program priorities.

### 10.4 Recommended Actions — Near-Term (30–90 Days)

1. **Complete PCB review** of this PMP Update; obtain formal PCB decision on the gated rate increase framework.
2. **Revise all eight affected program documents** per Section 2.3 within 60 days of PCB approval.
3. **Issue supplier contract modification packages** to Tier 1 suppliers requiring formal volume commitments at 6/year.
4. **Conduct independent MRL assessment** against SAE AS2805 criteria at the planned LRIP-2 target rate; document findings as Gate 7 evidence.
5. **Re-validate PRB Constraint C-1** ($21.5M FRP unit price) against revised learning curve model; formally re-baseline if the model changes.
6. **Establish learning curve tracking dashboard** with monthly reporting and automatic rate freeze triggers per Section 5.4.
7. **Negotiate MAAP-1F variant contract modification** with wildfire fleet customers for configuration-specific NRE cost recovery.

### 10.5 Decision Framework Summary

| Decision Gate | Condition | Authorized Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Immediate — PCB Pending** | Demand exists; contracts not yet signed; readiness unconfirmed | Begin supplier capacity reservation; initiate workforce pipeline; do not authorize tooling investment |
| **LRIP-1 Complete — Gate Review** | All 8 gate criteria in Section 3.4 satisfied | Authorize rate increase to 3–4 aircraft/year for LRIP-2 as intermediate step |
| **LRIP-2 Mid-Point — Gate Review** | Learning curve ≤87% sustained; all quality KPIs met; firm contracts for 6+ additional aircraft in hand | Authorize rate increase to 6 aircraft/year for balance of LRIP-2 or LRIP-3 |
| **Any Point — Rate Freeze Trigger** | Learning curve >88% sustained two months; NCR >1%; any Gate criterion failed | Freeze rate at preceding level; mandatory corrective action before rate restoration |
| **Any Point — Absolute Constraint** | MAAP-1F certification not closed; OT&E major findings open; MRL <8 | No LRIP-2 deliveries to wildfire operators regardless of rate authority status |

The correct answer to this proposed change is not "no." It is "not yet, not all at once, and not without the readiness that protects both the program and the customers it is committed to serve." The wildfire fleet demand is real. The path to meeting it runs through demonstrated quality and manufacturing maturity — not around them.
